text.title: usability-testing Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for usability-testing. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: State test goals as answerable questions, not topics. Each question must be tied to a measurable metric.
rule.rationale: Vague goals ("understand navigation") produce unactionable findings. Specific questions ("Can users complete checkout in under 3 minutes?") produce pass/fail evidence.

rule.statement: Document objectives, participant profile, tasks, metrics, and environment before recruiting. Include session duration, success criteria per task, and the analysis method (moderated vs. unmoderated).
rule.rationale: Recruiting before task design means participants may not match the tasks. Plan first, recruit second. A written plan prevents scope creep and ensures consistency across sessions.

rule.statement: Tasks must use realistic scenarios with clear goals and zero hints about the solution path. Task format: Scenario: [context and motivation]. Task: [what to accomplish]. Success: [measurable completion].
rule.rationale: Hints in task wording ("click the Account button") inflate task success rates and produce false confidence in design quality. Scenario framing activates realistic decision-making; decontextualized tasks feel artificial.

rule.statement: Never include UI element names, menu labels, or step sequences in a task instruction. Do not say "Click the menu and select Orders"; instead say "Find your recent order and update the shipping address".
rule.rationale: Including solution steps measures comprehension of instructions, not usability of the interface.

rule.statement: Recruit 5-8 participants per distinct user segment; match the target user profile including experience level mix. Segments with different mental models must be tested separately (e.g., iOS users and Android users if you support both platforms).
rule.rationale: Fewer than 5 participants per segment misses recurring patterns. Fewer than 5 also means one outlier skews results. Five participants reveal approximately 85% of major usability problems (Nielsen research).

rule.statement: Facilitate without helping: do not answer questions, guide navigation, or react to errors during tasks. If a user is stuck, say "There is no right answer; try what feels natural" and wait. If they truly cannot proceed, mark the task as failed and move on.
rule.rationale: Any facilitator intervention invalidates the task result — it measures the facilitator's skill, not the product's usability. Patience and silence are core skills for a usability moderator.

rule.statement: Assign severity ratings (Critical/High/Medium/Low) to every finding before reporting. Severity definition: Critical = users cannot complete the task; High = significant difficulty completing; Medium = some struggle; Low = minor friction.
rule.rationale: Unrated findings produce undifferentiated lists that teams cannot prioritize. Severity ratings force triage and align effort with impact.

rule.statement: Each finding must include: severity, affected task, frequency (N of Y participants), problem description, direct quote or evidence, and a concrete recommendation.
rule.rationale: Findings without recommendations leave design decisions to whoever reads the report. Researchers must propose the fix to ensure findings drive action.

rule.statement: Use moderated sessions for complex, exploratory research where follow-up probing is needed; use unmoderated for validation at scale. Moderated: facilitator present, can probe, fewer sessions, higher cost, deeper insight. Unmoderated: remote automated, pre-set questions only, scales to many sessions, lower cost, broader patterns.
rule.rationale: Moderated sessions surface the why behind behavior; unmoderated provides statistical breadth. Choosing the wrong format wastes budget.
